retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gastric Intrinsic Factor -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Ado-Cbl is a cofactor for methylmalonyl CoA mutase enzyme. In this reaction, the methylmalonyl-CoA (a product from odd number fatty acid catabolism) converted to succinyl-CoA. The latter makes its way into two pathways: the citric acid cycle and heme synthesis. Accumulation of methylmalonyl-CoA responsible for: Neurological disorders due to the incorporation of methylmalonyl-CoA into the myelin sheath, and the result is neuron demyelination. Methylmalonic aciduria

retrieved_docs.title: Mean Corpuscular Volume -- History and Physical -- Mean Corpuscular Volume Below 80 fL
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with lead exposure are frequently exposed to lead paints, notably children in older homes where lead-based paints are common. Adults with lead exposure may report occupational exposures such as mining, pipefitting, auto repair work, and ceramic glazing. Lead poisoning can present with characteristic lead lines on the gums and metaphyses of long bones, along with symptoms such as abdominal pain, peripheral neuropathy (most commonly affecting the fibular and radial nerves), and fatigue. In severe cases, children can develop encephalopathy. Sideroblastic anemia can occur as a result of lead poisoning or chronic alcohol use, serving as the underlying etiology in some cases. Studies have shown a correlation between a low (pre-surgical) MCV and advanced stages (III or IV) of endometriosis. [18] These lower indices are believed to reflect dysregulated iron metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Nitrous oxide inactivates vitamin B12 by oxidation of the cobalt ion in cobalamin and makes the vitamin B12 functionally inactive. As a consequence, the methionine synthase activity is reduced. Reduced methionine results in myelin maintenance failure and secondary axonal degeneration in SCD. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxocobalamin -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: One of the neurological disorders associated with B12 deficiency is subacute combined degeneration of the cord (SCD), a condition characterized by demyelination of the dorsal and lateral columns of the spinal cord. B12 plays a vital role in myelin production. The active form of B12 (adenosylcobalamin) serves as a cofactor in converting methylmalonyl-CoA to succinyl-CoA, an essential step in lipid synthesis. Without adenosylcobalamin, abnormal fatty acids incorporate into neuronal lipids interfering with normal myelin formation. Additionally, B12 deficiency can cause abnormal DNA synthesis, potentially hindering oligodendrocyte growth, adversely affecting myelin production.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: SCD can be caused by a functional deficit of vitamin B12 secondary to repeated use of nitrous oxide. Nitrous oxide inactivates vitamin B12 by oxidizing the cobalt in the vitamin B12 molecule. The functionally deficient vitamin B12 causes impaired methionine synthase activity, which leads to impaired myelin maintenance and axonal degeneration.

retrieved_docs.title: Superficial Siderosis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Due to persistent and recurring bleeding into the subarachnoid space, superficial siderosis results from the toxic buildup of hemosiderin on the surface of the brain and pia mater from the circulating cerebrospinal fluid (CSF). In the CSF, hemoglobin in red blood cells is broken down into globin and neurotoxic heme. Bergman glia and microglia cells are conduits for iron entry into the tissue, facilitating ferritin biosynthesis. Neuroglial cells produce heme oxygenase and apoferritin. [1] Heme oxygenase breaks down heme into free iron and biliverdin. Apoferritin binds the free iron produced from heme oxygenase to form ferritin and, subsequently, hemosiderin. The persistence of blood in the subarachnoid space eventually overwhelms the ferritin production capacity, and overloading this pathway results in subsequent free radical damage and lipid peroxidation-mediated neuronal injury. [1] [11] Hemosiderin causes gliosis, demyelination, and neuronal loss, causing permanent damage to the involved structures except for the olfactory nerve. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Synapse -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Once myelinated, the intermediate zone forms gray matter, while the nerve processes that make up the marginal zone become white matter. The neurons must then differentiate from their precursors. The order in which they do this is based on their size, with the largest neurons (motor neurons) differentiating first. Around the time of birth, the smaller neurons (sensory neurons) develop, along with glial cells. Glial cells aid in the differentiation of the neurons and facilitate their growth in the direction of their target locations. Later, glial cells participate in the reuptake of excess neurotransmitters in the synaptic cleft.

retrieved_docs.title: Superficial Siderosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Superficial siderosis is a rare neurological disorder characterized by iron deposition in the superficial layers of the brain and spinal cord. This condition typically arises from chronic bleeding into the subarachnoid space, often due to previous head or spinal trauma, tumors, or vascular malformations. As iron accumulates over time, it damages nerve tissues, leading to a spectrum of symptoms ranging from progressive hearing loss and imbalance to cognitive decline and neurological deficits. Despite its rarity, superficial siderosis presents a diagnostic challenge, often mimicking other more common neurological conditions. Understanding its pathophysiology, clinical manifestations, and management strategies is essential for early recognition and intervention to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Tay-Sachs Disease -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Gangliosides are the major glycolipid of the neuronal cell membrane, ensuring normal cellular activity. [9] Ganglioside expression in the brain is highly region-specific, highly regulated, and correlated with neurodevelopmental milestones, including neural tube formation, neuritogenesis, axonogenesis, synaptogenesis, and myelination. Ganglioside accretion occurs as early as the 10th week of gestation and continues through 5 years of life. Ganglioside expression also plays a significant role in modulating ion channel and receptor signaling, ensuring optimum function and adaption of neuronal circuits involved in neurotransmission, memory, and learning.

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Combined Degeneration of the Spinal Cord(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12 plays a vital role in deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) synthesis and odd-chain fatty acid metabolism, which are required to maintain the integrity of neuronal myelin. The vitamin is a cofactor for the following enzymes:

retrieved_docs.title: Mean Corpuscular Volume -- History and Physical -- Mean Corpuscular Volume Above 100 fL
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with MCV above 100 fL often exhibit signs of subacute combined degeneration, including cerebellar ataxia, bilateral hemiplegia, decreased vibration, and discriminative touch sensations. Folate deficiency may manifest with glossitis as a prominent symptom but typically lacks other specific symptoms. However, pregnant women with folate deficiency are at risk of fetal neural tube defects such as spina bifida occulta, especially if they do not use supplemental vitamins during pregnancy. Chronic alcoholism can contribute to folate deficiency, adding to the risk in such cases. Orotic aciduria typically presents early in life and is characterized by failure to thrive and delayed development, particularly in patients with a family history of the condition. Non-megaloblastic anemias often stem from underlying causes such as hepatic insufficiency, which can also be associated with chronic alcoholism, a prevalent contributing factor.

retrieved_docs.title: Metachromatic Leukodystrophy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Leukodystrophies are inherited disorders that predominantly affect the central nervous system (CNS) white matter tracts, and it's cellular components. These may include glial cells, myelin sheath, and axons. Genetic leukodystrophies tend to combine features of leukodystrophies with development issues caused by inborn errors of metabolism, disorders of DNA transcription, translation, production of critical CNS proteins including myelin, and neuronal cytoskeletal dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Posterior Column (Dorsal Column) -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In a different pathway, the lack of vitamin B12 impedes oligodendrocyte growth, as vitamin B12 is involved in DNA synthesis and is a cofactor in the generation of tetrahydrofolate. Other vitamin B12 deficiency findings besides SCD include psychiatric issues and macrocytic anemia. Vitamin B12 deficiency can also be secondary to folate deficiency, methotrexate therapy, and nitric oxide intake. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Combined Degeneration of the Spinal Cord(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Methylmalonyl-coenzyme A mutase: This enzyme converts methylmalonyl-coenzyme A (CoA) to succinyl-CoA, which is required for myelin synthesis. Interruption of this step leads to the accumulation of methylmalonyl-CoA and propionyl-CoA, which disrupts normal myelin synthesis and accumulates abnormal fatty acids.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: SCD can be caused by the use and misuse of nitrous oxide. [2] It can also be caused by copper deficiency resulting from excessive zinc consumption.[3] These are less common yet important causes to recognize in clinical practice. Unexplained anemia, coupled with neurological symptoms, should raise suspicions of SCD, and an evaluation investigating vitamin B12 or, less commonly, copper deficiency in susceptible populations should be initiated.

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Combined Degeneration of the Spinal Cord(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Homocysteine methyltransferase: This enzyme carries out the conversion of homocysteine to methionine. Methionine is a precursor for S-adenosyl methionine, a methyl donor required to maintain the integrity of the neuron sheath. A deficiency of vitamin B12 disrupts this reaction, which leads to reduced formation of S- adenosyl methionine. This impairs the methylation of myelin basic protein and lipids and damages the myelin sheath. Homocysteine methyltransferase also catalyzes the conversion of 5-methyl-tetrahydrofolate to tetrahydrofolate, which acts as a one-carbon donor for DNA synthesis. A deficiency of vitamin B12 causes folate to be trapped in its methylated form of 5-methyl-tetrahydrofolate.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2040
retrieved_docs.content: FOLIC ACID/IRON/MULTIVITAMS is an oral delayed‑release capsule formulation that combines a broad spectrum of vitamins, minerals, amino‑acid derivatives, co‑enzymes and specialized lipid compounds. The product’s ingredient profile includes leucovorin (2.5 mg) and levomefolate magnesium (7 mg) as active forms of folate, as well as folic acid (1 mg) and cobamamide (50 µg) for vitamin B12 activity. B‑vitamin components also comprise pyridoxal phosphate (25 µg), niacin (3 mg), flavin adenine dinucleotide (25 µg) and beta‑alanine‑derived betaine (500 µg). Iron is supplied in multiple bioavailable forms: ferrous cysteine glycinate (13.6 mg), ferrous fumarate (62.5 mg) and a polysaccharide iron complex (62.5 mg). Magnesium is provided as magnesium ascorbate (24 mg) and magnesium L‑threonate (1 mg), while zinc is present as zinc ascorbate (1 mg). Additional antioxidant and metabolic support is offered by ascorbic acid (40 mg) and NADH (25 µg). The formulation also contains specialized phospholipid derivatives—1,2‑docosahexanoyl‑SN‑glycero‑3‑phosphoserine calcium (6.4 mg) and 1,2‑icosapentoyl‑SN‑glycero‑3‑phosphoserine calcium (800 µg)—as well as phosphatidyl‑serine (12 mg) for neuronal health. Supporting agents include citric acid monohydrate (1.83 mg), sodium citrate (3.67 mg), and co‑enzyme‑like cocarboxylase (25 µg). Together, these ingredients provide a comprehensive multivitamin and mineral supplement aimed at supporting hematologic function, metabolic processes, antioxidant defenses, and neurological health in patients requiring folic acid, iron and broad micronutrient supplementation.

The integrated composition is intended for patients with iron‑deficiency anemia, folate deficiency, or increased nutritional needs, providing a convenient single‑dose regimen that addresses several micronutrient deficiencies simultaneously.

By incorporating folic acid into multivitamin‑iron supplements, clinicians aim to correct or avert folate deficiency, support normal erythropoiesis, and improve overall hematologic health while simultaneously providing other essential vitamins (A, C, D, E, B‑complex) and minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iodine) that contribute to broader metabolic and physiological functions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 382
retrieved_docs.content: provides multiple iron salts (ferrous fumarate, polysaccharide iron complex, ferrous cysteine glycinate) together with vitamin C derivatives (ascorbic acid, magnesium ascorbate, zinc ascorbate) that facilitate iron uptake, and a spectrum of B‑vitamins and cofactors that support erythropoiesis and overall metabolic health. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how specific oral iron preparations—whether bivalent iron‑ascorbate salts or broader multivitamin‑iron combinations—are organized within therapeutic classes and formulated to maximize iron bioavailability while addressing concurrent micronutrient deficiencies commonly seen in patients with iron deficiency anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 5 -- Community 2929
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymology, the scientific study of enzymes and their biochemical actions, is recognized as a relevant qualifier for several distinct medical topics. In the context of Cannabis, enzymology helps elucidate how the plant’s cannabinoids are metabolized, how enzyme‑mediated pathways influence its pharmacodynamics, and how enzymatic interactions may affect both therapeutic benefits and adverse effects. Enzymology also provides essential insight into فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد (iron‑deficiency anemia), where enzyme‑dependent processes such as heme synthesis, iron transport, and erythropoiesis are central to the disorder’s pathophysiology and to the development of targeted diagnostic and therapeutic strategies. Additionally, enzymology is pertinent to gout and other crystal arthropathies, because enzyme‑driven metabolic pathways—particularly those governing purine turnover, uric acid production, and crystal formation—underlie the onset and progression of these conditions. By integrating enzymological knowledge across Cannabis use, iron‑deficiency anemia, and gout‑related crystal arthropathies, clinicians and researchers can achieve a more comprehensive understanding of disease mechanisms, improve diagnostic accuracy, and tailor interventions that consider the underlying enzymatic processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2880
retrieved_docs.content: Cannabis is recognized as an allowed qualifier in both embryology and enzymology. Embryology and enzymology are each allowed qualifiers for a set of medical conditions that include iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد), cystinuria (بيلة سِّيسْتينِية), gout and other crystal arthropathies, cerebral palsy (فالج), and ulcers of the lower limbs (قرحات الطرفين السفليين). This interconnection indicates that discussions of these disorders can appropriately reference either embryological or enzymological perspectives, and that Cannabis may be considered within the same qualifying framework when related to embryology or enzymology.

retrieved_docs.content: FERROUS AMINO ACID CHELATE is a pharmaceutical preparation that combines iron with folic acid and is classified as a member of the iron‑in‑combination‑with‑folic‑acid group, reflecting its role in providing both elemental iron and folate for the treatment of iron deficiency and support of folate-dependent metabolic processes.
